title: Box Breathing (4-4-4-4)
description: Box Breathing is a powerful stress-relief technique used by Navy SEALs and first responders. It activates your parasympathetic nervous system, slowing your heart rate and promoting a state of calm.
instructions: Find a comfortable seated position and close your eyes.
Inhale slowly through your nose for 4 seconds, feeling your lungs expand fully.
Hold your breath gently for 4 seconds — don't clench, just pause.
Exhale slowly through your mouth for 4 seconds, releasing all tension.
Hold your breath again for 4 seconds before beginning the next cycle.
Repeat for 4-6 cycles or until you feel a sense of calm wash over you.
scientific_basis: Controlled breathing stimulates the vagus nerve, which activates the parasympathetic nervous system and reduces cortisol levels.

title: Body Scan Meditation
description: A guided journey through your body that helps you notice and release physical tension. This practice builds mind-body awareness and is especially effective before sleep.
instructions: Lie down in a comfortable position or sit with your back supported.
Close your eyes and take three deep, cleansing breaths.
Begin at the top of your head — notice any sensations without judgment.
Slowly move your attention down: forehead, eyes, jaw, neck, shoulders.
Continue scanning through your arms, chest, abdomen, hips, legs, and feet.
At each area, breathe into any tension you find and consciously release it.
After reaching your feet, take a moment to feel your whole body at once.
Gently open your eyes when you're ready.
scientific_basis: Body scan meditation has been shown to reduce symptoms of anxiety, depression, and chronic pain by enhancing interoceptive awareness (Ditto et al., 2006).

title: Gratitude Journaling
description: Gratitude journaling rewires your brain to focus on positive aspects of life. Research shows that writing down things you're grateful for can significantly boost happiness and reduce depressive symptoms.
instructions: Set aside 10 quiet minutes — morning or evening works best.
Write down 3 specific things you are grateful for today.
For each item, write WHY you are grateful — be specific and personal.
Reflect on one person who positively impacted your day and describe how.
Close by writing one kind thing you did for yourself or others today.
Read your entries aloud if comfortable — hearing gratitude amplifies its effect.
scientific_basis: Emmons & McCullough (2003) demonstrated that gratitude journaling increases well-being by 25% and improves sleep quality.

title: 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding Technique
description: This sensory-based grounding exercise anchors you to the present moment by engaging all five senses. It's one of the most effective techniques for interrupting anxiety spirals and panic attacks.
instructions: Pause whatever you're doing and take one slow, deep breath.
Name 5 things you can SEE — look around and describe them in detail.
Name 4 things you can TOUCH — feel textures around you (clothing, desk, skin).
Name 3 things you can HEAR — listen carefully for ambient sounds.
Name 2 things you can SMELL — notice scents in your environment.
Name 1 thing you can TASTE — notice the taste in your mouth right now.
Finish with three slow breaths and check in with how you feel.
scientific_basis: Grounding techniques reduce amygdala activation and engage the prefrontal cortex, helping regain cognitive control during fight-or-flight responses.

title: Loving-Kindness Meditation (Metta)
description: Loving-Kindness Meditation (Metta) cultivates feelings of warmth and goodwill — first toward yourself, then extending outward to others. It's a powerful antidote to self-criticism and interpersonal conflict.
instructions: Sit comfortably, close your eyes, and place a hand over your heart.
Begin by directing kindness to yourself. Silently repeat: 'May I be happy. May I be healthy. May I be safe. May I live with ease.'
Visualize someone you love. Repeat the phrases for them: 'May you be happy...'
Think of a neutral person (a stranger or acquaintance). Extend the same wishes to them.
If you're ready, think of someone difficult. Offer them the same phrases with compassion.
Finally, extend loving-kindness to all beings everywhere: 'May all beings be happy...'
Sit quietly for a moment and notice the warmth in your chest.
scientific_basis: Fredrickson et al. (2008) found that 7 weeks of Loving-Kindness Meditation increased positive emotions, social connectedness, and life satisfaction.
